# ADR-260011: Connect public-site authentication to the GraphQL backend with Google Sign-In support

Status: Accepted
Date: 2026-06-10
Owners: Repository owner

## Context

The current sign-in form on `crystord.com` POSTs email and password to a REST-like endpoint
(`PUBLIC_APP_SIGN_IN_URL`). The backend has since exposed a GraphQL API that covers all auth
operations in one schema:

- `query signin(email, password): String!` — returns a JWT on success
- `mutation signup(email, password, username): String!` — creates an account, returns JWT
- `query signinGoogle(idToken: String!): String!` — exchanges a Google ID token for a JWT
- `query getGoogleClientID: String!` — returns the OAuth client ID for the GSI button

Sign-up currently lives entirely at `app.crystord.com/sign-up` and is unreachable from the public
site without a cross-origin redirect. Google Sign-In is defined in the backend schema but absent
from the frontend. A demo user (`demo` / `demo`) exists for frictionless product evaluation.

The public site should expose the full auth surface — sign-in, sign-up, Google SSO, and demo
access — while remaining a fully static Astro build with no server runtime.

## Decision

### 1. GraphQL as the single auth transport

All auth operations call the GraphQL endpoint configured via `PUBLIC_GRAPHQL_URL`
(default: `https://app.crystord.com/graphql`). Requests use vanilla `fetch` with the default
credentials mode (omit `credentials` option entirely — bearer-token auth requires no cookies).
The body follows the standard GraphQL over HTTP format (`{ query, variables }`), with `variables`
omitted when the operation takes no arguments. No GraphQL client library is introduced.

`PUBLIC_APP_SIGN_IN_URL` and `PUBLIC_APP_SIGN_UP_URL` are retained as the `href` target for the
sign-in nav link and as no-JS `<form action>` fallbacks; they are no longer the XHR target.

### 2. JWT handoff via query parameter

`signin`, `signup`, and `signinGoogle` each return a JWT string. After a successful call the
frontend navigates to `app.crystord.com` with the token as a query parameter:
`https://app.crystord.com/?token=<jwt>`. This is a full browser navigation (assignment to
`window.location.href`), not a fetch, so no cross-origin request is involved. The app reads
the query parameter on first load, bootstraps the session, and removes the parameter from the
URL. The redirect target is derived from `new URL(graphqlUrl).origin` so it stays in sync with
`PUBLIC_GRAPHQL_URL` across environments.

### 3. Signup form on the public site

A `/sign-up` page is added following the vertical-slice convention (ADR-260010):
`src/features/sign-up/`. The form calls `mutation signup` and redirects on success using the
same JWT fragment pattern. The username field is optional, matching the schema.

### 4. Google Sign-In via GSI

The Google Identity Services library (`accounts.google.com/gsi/client`) is loaded on the
sign-in and sign-up pages only. The OAuth client ID is fetched at page load via
`query getGoogleClientID`. The Google button renders after the client ID resolves; it is omitted
silently if the query fails, so the email/password path always remains functional.

The GSI library is an authentication-only external resource. It does not fall under the
analytics-script or form-backend prohibition stated in ADR-260004. The boundary tests are
extended to explicitly permit `accounts.google.com/gsi/client` while continuing to block
analytics and form-backend patterns.

### 5. Demo access

A "Try Demo" button on the sign-in form pre-fills and submits credentials `demo` / `demo`
through the standard `signin` path. The credentials are hardcoded constants in the utility
layer, not exposed in markup.

### 6. Utility structure

New TypeScript modules under `src/utils/`:

| Module | Responsibility |
|--------|----------------|
| `auth-graphql.ts` | `gqlSignIn`, `gqlSignUp`, `gqlSignInGoogle`, `getGoogleClientId` — thin wrappers over `fetch` |
| `google-auth.ts` | GSI initialisation helper: loads the script tag, waits for `google.accounts`, renders the button |

The existing `sign-in.ts` utility is retained for backward compatibility of the sign-in form
until the migration is complete; `auth-graphql.ts` supersedes its API call.

## Alternatives Considered

- **Apollo or URQL GraphQL client**: Rejected. The surface is narrow (four operations) and the
  site is static. A client library adds ≥30 kB and build complexity that is not justified.
- **Vendor the GSI library at build time**: Google's terms require loading the script from
  `accounts.google.com` to receive security patches automatically. Vendoring is not permitted.
- **Combined sign-in/sign-up tab UI on one page**: Works but increases component complexity and
  departs from the established one-page-per-route pattern. Separate routes keep each slice
  testable and navigable independently.
- **Pass JWT as a URL fragment (`#token=`)**: Fragments are not sent to the server, but the
  app is on the same origin as the API (`app.crystord.com`) so server-log exposure is not the
  concern here. The query parameter approach is simpler and easier for the app to read and
  clear without triggering a second navigation.
- **Set a cross-origin cookie**: Not applicable. The GraphQL API is on `app.crystord.com` and
  the auth calls from `crystord.com` are plain cross-origin fetch requests (no credentials mode
  required for bearer-token auth).

## Consequences

- Positive: All auth operations use a single typed GraphQL API; the REST sign-in endpoint is
  no longer needed for the public site.
- Positive: Google Sign-In reduces friction for the core user segment (Google Workspace users).
- Positive: Demo access makes product evaluation one click away without account creation.
- Positive: Signup is available on the public site, removing the detour to `app.crystord.com`.
- Negative: `getGoogleClientID` is a runtime network call; the Google button does not render
  until it resolves (~one round-trip). The email/password path is unaffected.
- Negative: The GSI script is a third-party resource on auth pages; it introduces a content
  security policy consideration for future CSP header configuration.
- Risk: If the GraphQL endpoint URL changes, `PUBLIC_GRAPHQL_URL` must be updated at build time.
  The env var default must be kept in sync with the backend deployment.
- Note: No `credentials: "include"` or CORS credential configuration is required. Auth uses
  bearer tokens; the JWT redirect is a navigation, not a fetch.

## Verification

- Email/password sign-in succeeds with valid credentials and redirects with a JWT fragment.
- Email/password sign-in fails inline without a full page reload on invalid credentials.
- Signup creates an account and redirects with a JWT fragment.
- Google Sign-In completes end-to-end with a real Google token.
- "Try Demo" button signs in with `demo`/`demo` and redirects.
- `PUBLIC_GRAPHQL_URL` is the GraphQL target; it is not hardcoded in any component.
- Unit test coverage is 100% on `auth-graphql.ts` and `google-auth.ts`.
- All existing boundary tests pass; the GSI origin is explicitly listed as permitted.
- No regression on sign-in form behaviour (existing Playwright e2e suite passes).

## Rollback

Revert `SignInForm.astro` to use `submitSignIn` from `sign-in.ts` and remove `auth-graphql.ts`,
`google-auth.ts`, and the `/sign-up` page. Restore `PUBLIC_APP_SIGN_IN_URL` as the XHR target
in `SignInForm.astro`. No routing changes are needed; no backend changes are required.
